summary: Decompiled the Ness Yo-Yo item callback it_2725_Logic59_EvtUnk in src/melee/it/items/itnessyoyo.c, replacing the MWERKS_GEKKO inline assembly and NOT_IMPLEMENTED fallback with matching C. The important matching detail was preserving an otherwise unused comparison after it_8026B894 by reading xDD4_itemVar.nessyoyo.x10 through a volatile pointer and comparing it to ref_gobj. decomp.dev reported GALE01 had no changes.
what_changed: Removed the MWERKS_GEKKO inline assembly implementation of it_2725_Logic59_EvtUnk.
Removed the non-Gekko NOT_IMPLEMENTED body for it_2725_Logic59_EvtUnk.
Added a C implementation that obtains Item* ip with GET_ITEM(gobj), calls it_8026B894(gobj, ref_gobj), then performs a no-op comparison involving ip->xDD4_itemVar.nessyoyo.x10 and ref_gobj.
Net diff was small: one changed file with 6 added lines and 32 deleted lines.
smart_moves: Matched a tricky dead comparison by using HSD_GObj* volatile* owner_ptr = &ip->xDD4_itemVar.nessyoyo.x10 before comparing *owner_ptr to ref_gobj.
Used a cast-to-void ternary expression, ((*owner_ptr == ref_gobj) ? 0 : 0), to keep the comparison side effect in generated code without changing runtime behavior.
Kept the semantic call to it_8026B894(gobj, ref_gobj) before the unused comparison, matching the assembly order.
decomp_lessons: When an assembly function performs a comparison whose result is not consumed, matching C may need an artificial volatile read/comparison to prevent the compiler from optimizing it away.
A volatile pointer to a struct field can be a targeted way to force a load from a known item variable field without making the whole object volatile.
For item callbacks, preserve call order around helper functions such as it_8026B894 before introducing matching-only no-op expressions.
Dead comparisons in original code can be evidence of compiler artifacts or unused logic; document them with a minimal matching construct rather than inventing gameplay behavior.
naming_conventions: Preserved the existing autogenerated/item callback symbol name it_2725_Logic59_EvtUnk.
Used standard project item types and helpers: Item_GObj, HSD_GObj, Item, and GET_ITEM.
Accessed the Ness Yo-Yo item variable through the existing nested field path xDD4_itemVar.nessyoyo.x10.
Local variable names used in the C implementation were concise and conventional for this codebase: ip for Item* and owner_ptr for the compared GObj pointer field.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: The original assembly loaded the item data from gobj, called it_8026B894, loaded the field at the corresponding item-var offset, compared it with ref_gobj, and returned without using the comparison result.
The replacement C mirrors that pattern by retrieving the item, making the helper call, then forcing the field load and comparison.
The volatile pointer and no-op ternary are the key tactic for emitting an otherwise unused cmplw-style comparison.
Removing the #ifdef MWERKS_GEKKO asm/NOT_IMPLEMENTED split simplified the function to a single C implementation.
review_feedback: No human review comments were present in the slice.
The PR body notes: "The unused comparison made this one a bit tricky, but it matches 100% on my end."
decomp.dev bot reported for GALE01: "No changes".
follow_up_queries: Find other item callbacks with unused comparisons after helper calls that may need volatile pointer matching tactics.
Search for remaining MWERKS_GEKKO inline asm blocks in src/melee/it/items.
Search for NOT_IMPLEMENTED in Ness Yo-Yo item logic.
Compare other uses of xDD4_itemVar.nessyoyo.x10 to confirm whether owner_ptr is an appropriate semantic name.
